transcript.transcript: Okay, Barbara, welcome. I want to welcome our listeners to an interview today with Barbara Westcott. And this interview is part of the America 250 celebration. And it is Idaho's contribution to oral histories for our state to commemorate Idaho's journey in the American legacy. And in particular, Barbara's going to be sharing early dance history of the Treasure Valley. So welcome, Barbara. Thank you. Yeah, I want you to please acknowledge that, you know, that we are recording this conversation and that we have given you, um, a notice of this, and we will be sending a verbal, uh, written consent form to you. Yes, I understand, okay. And, uh, my name is Alida Sales Pottenger. And joining me today is Barbara Westcott. Barbara, could you please state your full name and please spell it? Oh, okay. It's Barbara Westcott and the last name is w e s c o TT. Very good. And your maiden name? It was rice. Like we eat rice. Mhm. And you spell your name the traditional. Barbara. I do. Fantastic. Uh, today's date is November 4th, 2025. And, uh, the location of our interview is Barbara's speaking from her home in Boise, and I'm in the Indianapolis, Indiana area. And today, we'll be preserving the voice, the memories, the perspectives and the unique history in Barbara's own words. So, Barbara, welcome. This is going to be fun. Thank you. Yeah. All right, so, uh, obviously, your connection to dance and the state of Idaho is profound. Um, I knew about you when I was a girl growing up in Boise, OAC. And I first learned more about your life story when you invited me to your home in August of 2024. Uh, I got to attend a beautiful lunch at your house, along with, um, uh, Kendra Jesse, who was one of my beloved dance teachers and another fellow dance friend of yours, K. And can you refresh my memory what Kay's full name is? Her name is Kay Braden b r a d fabulous. And I, I met her when we were both ballet students at the University of Utah. Okay. And how did you become friends with Kay or Jesse? I think that, well, Lloyd Carlton, who was the longtime dance teacher in Boise, uh, had helped organize the Boise Valley Dance Teachers Association. And in those days, although all of us were competitors, we were also very good friends, and we met every month for breakfast and coffee and discussed what was going on in dance and shared information about our students. And that's where I met K and K and I became good friends and continue to be good friends to this day. That is such a great story about camaraderie and, uh, what an integral part of dance Boise was or dance was in Boise. Um, you also, uh, knew Pat Harris, who just recently died in September. Um, can you tell me about, uh, a little bit about Pat? Well, after I was when I was no longer a student, then there became more dance schools in the Boise area, and Pat's came back from the University of Washington with a degree in physical education and started her studio in Boise, which probably became one of the largest schools in Boise at that time. Um, she had built a building, had a building all of her own, not having to rent from space from someone else. And, and I think she probably had the most students of anybody of probably through the 70s. 80s. Um, yeah. I was a student at Pat's, as you know, as well. And, um, she, uh, definitely made a huge contribution to my life as a teacher and a mentor, and, uh, I'm dearly miss her. So, uh, tell me about your life growing up. Where were you born, Barbara? I'm one of the very few people that was born in Boise. Most everyone else seems to have come from somewhere else, and I began taking ballet classes from Lloyd Carlton in 1951, 81. After I saw a performance of the Ballet Russe at what was then the Pinney Theatre, uh, on, I think it was on Jefferson in Boise. And at that time, the National Ballet companies toured in a bus, and we were the stop between Salt Lake and Portland. So we got to see some pretty fabulous entertainment just because that was the overnight stop. I was so enthralled with that performance that I'm sure that the very next day I started begging to take ballet classes. And so I started when I was eight from taking classes from Lloyd Carlton, who at that time was renting space in what is known as the Gar Hall, which is a building on the historical register next door to Saint Michael's Cathedral. Um, just a one room studio. That's all it was. Wow. Very interesting. You know, after you and I talked a couple of weeks ago about the Penny theater, I was, you know, it brought back memories. And so I certainly did a little research on that. And, uh, you may know this, but the Penny theater was built by a man named James Penny in 1908. And, um, he was actually quite famous because he was the mayor of Boise. Uh, five different, um, times and, uh, uh, separated in between with other mayors. And many consider him the, um, I guess you would call it senior mayor of our community, which was really cool. I didn't realize that he had such an interest in theater. Um, I think the Penny theater. Well, and perhaps the Ada also. But the Penny theater was unique. And maybe in other cities they were similar at that time, but they were built with a performance stage, whereas now it just has a screen. And so the penny had a had a balcony and the stage was big enough for performances. And a little side note is his great granddaughter is a friend of mine. Um, and she carries his name. Her middle name is Penny. Mary. Penny. Oh, wow. Was she also a performer or an entertainment? No, she wasn't. She, um, no, she wasn't. And she had. So it was better for both of us to go to the University of Idaho, where I graduated, and I was so unhappy that I could not continue my dance education. So I just kind of ignored dance for ten years. I think you did a great job, by the way. After I taught for her for two years, then I started Dance Arts Academy, my own studio here in Boise. And how long did you own it? And then Julie Stevens, uh, was teaching adult tap at Pat Harris's studio, and I, I took some classes from Julie as well. And both experiences were so fun because tap wasn't something I've really done. Uh, obviously in a long time. But I have taken tap off and on probably 3 or 4 different times during my lifetime. And I am the world's worst. I just oh, my God, I just can't do it. Well, you're hard on yourself. I'm sure you. Are. You did excellent. Well, when I was teaching. Go ahead. When I was teaching in Seattle, the class right after I finished was an adult tap class. And so I would stay and take that tap class, and I thought, Barbara, why are you so bad at this? This is just terrible. This is so embarrassing. Oh, um, backing up a little bit, um, the woman that one of the women that comes to my Sunday class is Ann Sokolsky Basquill, and she has a studio in Meridian. I don't know how long she's been in the Treasure Valley, because I've only known her in the most recent years. But she has a fabulous history in dance. And I just don't know how much of it is is local. Um, and I don't know how long she's had that studio, but I can certainly give her your give you her contact information if you want to talk to her. I just have no idea if she's been in the Treasure Valley long or not. Right, right. Okay, well, that'd be great. Also, you know, Barbara, you can you can do some interviews if you know of people that might be willing to talk. Because you see how simple this is? I just pressed record on our phone and, um, and then we can take and download these into the Idaho State Archives software. And, um, we'll be able to record their, their stories as well, which I think is amazing. Um, first of all, let me thank you. I tell you, this was so much fun. And if you think of other things that we didn't talk about that you'd like to share, we can have a part two of our conversation. And, um, if there's anything that we forgot to talk about that you, uh, would like to add, that would be wonderful. And, well, you know, I think I think Boise needs to be aware of how much fabulous talent we've turned out. So many professional performers from Boise. Um, but not just in dance, but specifically what we're talking about. So many of our students have done just great things. What's the name? One of your compatriots. And I can't think of her name. She, I think is in Denver. Um, her parents had the had the pantry restaurant. Name was Lisa Holtz. Oh, right. Right, right. Um, she's another one that's just done fabulous things with her dance education. Um, and and I and in and in the reverse. A number of people have come to Boise that have had great professional careers. There are 2 or 3 people here that danced with New York City Ballet. Um, there are people here that have, like John Swarthout, who danced with Houston Ballet, with Eliot Feld. Um, we just got a huge dance community here. That and I'm not sure everyone's really aware of. Um. Agreed. Agreed. Agreed. Well, once again, I want to thank you for your time today. This truly is a gift that you've given us by sharing. You know, so much history of dance in Treasure Valley, and, um, I can't begin to thank you enough. Well, this has been fun. It's always fun to relive memories like this. It is. It is. Absolutely. I am going to be turning this recording off now, and we'll talk privately, just for a few minutes before we hang up. Okay.

lines.line: # Early Dance Community and Camaraderie

> Barbara describes the Boise Valley Dance Teachers Association, highlighting the camaraderie among dance teachers despite being competitors. Monthly meetings fostered friendships and collaboration, with Barbara recounting how she met and became close friends with fellow teachers like Kay Braden.

metadata.title: Early Dance Community and Camaraderie
metadata.synopsis: Barbara describes the Boise Valley Dance Teachers Association, highlighting the camaraderie among dance teachers despite being competitors. Monthly meetings fostered friendships and collaboration, with Barbara recounting how she met and became close friends with fellow teachers like Kay Braden.

lines.line: # Pat Harris and Her Studio

> Barbara discusses Pat Harris's return to Boise, her establishment of a prominent dance studio, and her significant influence on the local dance scene. Pat's studio became one of the largest in the area, with its own building and a large student base, especially during the 1970s and 1980s.

metadata.title: Pat Harris and Her Studio
metadata.synopsis: Barbara discusses Pat Harris's return to Boise, her establishment of a prominent dance studio, and her significant influence on the local dance scene. Pat's studio became one of the largest in the area, with its own building and a large student base, especially during the 1970s and 1980s.

lines.line: # Barbara's Childhood and First Dance Experiences

> Barbara shares her unique status as a Boise native and recounts her early exposure to ballet, beginning with classes from Lloyd Carlton in 1951 after being inspired by a Ballet Russe performance at the Pinney Theatre. She describes the studio's location and her initial excitement for dance.

metadata.title: Barbara's Childhood and First Dance Experiences
metadata.synopsis: Barbara shares her unique status as a Boise native and recounts her early exposure to ballet, beginning with classes from Lloyd Carlton in 1951 after being inspired by a Ballet Russe performance at the Pinney Theatre. She describes the studio's location and her initial excitement for dance.

lines.line: # Pinney Theatre and Boise's Cultural Scene

> The conversation delves into the history of the Pinney Theatre, its founder James Pinney, and its significance in Boise's cultural life. The theatre's unique design, including a performance stage and balcony, is discussed, along with personal connections to the Pinney family.

metadata.title: Pinney Theatre and Boise's Cultural Scene
metadata.synopsis: The conversation delves into the history of the Pinney Theatre, its founder James Pinney, and its significance in Boise's cultural life. The theatre's unique design, including a performance stage and balcony, is discussed, along with personal connections to the Pinney family.

lines.line: # Tuesday Musicale and Access to World-Class Performances

> Barbara highlights the role of the Tuesday Musicale organization in bringing world-class entertainment to Boise, including opera, dance, and orchestral performances. She explains how affordable season subscriptions made these cultural experiences accessible to the community.

metadata.title: Tuesday Musicale and Access to World-Class Performances
metadata.synopsis: Barbara highlights the role of the Tuesday Musicale organization in bringing world-class entertainment to Boise, including opera, dance, and orchestral performances. She explains how affordable season subscriptions made these cultural experiences accessible to the community.

lines.line: # Barbara's Education and Family Background

> Barbara describes her upbringing in Boise's North End, her progression through local schools, and her family's move to the bench. She shares details about her sister Kathy, who was also involved in dance, and provides insight into her family's educational and geographic background.

metadata.title: Barbara's Education and Family Background
metadata.synopsis: Barbara describes her upbringing in Boise's North End, her progression through local schools, and her family's move to the bench. She shares details about her sister Kathy, who was also involved in dance, and provides insight into her family's educational and geographic background.

lines.line: # College Years, Injury, and Transition to Teaching

> Barbara recounts her time at the University of Utah, her knee injury, and the impact it had on her dance aspirations. She describes her transfer to the University of Idaho, her disappointment at leaving dance, and her eventual return to the art as a teacher, despite initial fears and lack of formal training.

metadata.title: College Years, Injury, and Transition to Teaching
metadata.synopsis: Barbara recounts her time at the University of Utah, her knee injury, and the impact it had on her dance aspirations. She describes her transfer to the University of Idaho, her disappointment at leaving dance, and her eventual return to the art as a teacher, despite initial fears and lack of formal training.

lines.line: # Founding Dance Arts Academy and Teaching Philosophy

> Barbara discusses the founding of her own studio, Dance Arts Academy, and its longevity in Boise. She reflects on her identity as a dancer rather than a teacher, her initial struggles with teaching, and the joy she found in sharing dance with students over the years.

metadata.title: Founding Dance Arts Academy and Teaching Philosophy
metadata.synopsis: Barbara discusses the founding of her own studio, Dance Arts Academy, and its longevity in Boise. She reflects on her identity as a dancer rather than a teacher, her initial struggles with teaching, and the joy she found in sharing dance with students over the years.

lines.line: # Current Teaching and Lifestyle

> Barbara shares her current teaching schedule, noting that she teaches three days a week and takes summers off to travel. She explains her approach to balancing teaching with personal interests and her willingness to help students outside of regular classes.

metadata.title: Current Teaching and Lifestyle
metadata.synopsis: Barbara shares her current teaching schedule, noting that she teaches three days a week and takes summers off to travel. She explains her approach to balancing teaching with personal interests and her willingness to help students outside of regular classes.

lines.line: # Parents' Background and Civic Involvement

> Barbara describes her parents' origins in Missouri and Kansas, their move to Boise, and their active roles in the community. Her mother was a school teacher and volunteer, while her father was a business owner and civic leader involved in city council and the Idaho Legislature.

metadata.title: Parents' Background and Civic Involvement
metadata.synopsis: Barbara describes her parents' origins in Missouri and Kansas, their move to Boise, and their active roles in the community. Her mother was a school teacher and volunteer, while her father was a business owner and civic leader involved in city council and the Idaho Legislature.

lines.line: # Marriage, Children, and Family Achievements

> Barbara talks about her marriage and her two children, focusing on her daughter's journey to becoming a professional dancer with Pacific Northwest Ballet and Pittsburgh Ballet Theatre. She also describes her son's musical talents and his supportive role in her dance productions.

metadata.title: Marriage, Children, and Family Achievements
metadata.synopsis: Barbara talks about her marriage and her two children, focusing on her daughter's journey to becoming a professional dancer with Pacific Northwest Ballet and Pittsburgh Ballet Theatre. She also describes her son's musical talents and his supportive role in her dance productions.

lines.line: # Memories of Lloyd Carlton and Dance School Friendships

> Barbara reflects on the influence of Lloyd Carlton, her ballet teacher, and the deep friendships formed through years spent in dance school. She shares how these relationships have endured, with former classmates and students remaining close friends.

metadata.title: Memories of Lloyd Carlton and Dance School Friendships
metadata.synopsis: Barbara reflects on the influence of Lloyd Carlton, her ballet teacher, and the deep friendships formed through years spent in dance school. She shares how these relationships have endured, with former classmates and students remaining close friends.

lines.line: # Other Early Dance Teachers and Studios

> Barbara mentions other notable dance teachers in Boise, such as Dori Moon and the Travelsteads, describing their studios and unique practices like dancing on a drive-in restaurant roof and using tap shoes on pointe. She provides historical context for these teachers and their impact on the local dance scene.

metadata.title: Other Early Dance Teachers and Studios
metadata.synopsis: Barbara mentions other notable dance teachers in Boise, such as Dori Moon and the Travelsteads, describing their studios and unique practices like dancing on a drive-in restaurant roof and using tap shoes on pointe. She provides historical context for these teachers and their impact on the local dance scene.

lines.line: # Boise Dance Collection and Touring Experiences

> Barbara discusses the Boise Dance Collection, a collaborative touring group organized by Lloyd Carlton. She describes how students traveled to perform in southern Idaho schools, gaining valuable experience and independence, and exposing communities to various dance forms.

metadata.title: Boise Dance Collection and Touring Experiences
metadata.synopsis: Barbara discusses the Boise Dance Collection, a collaborative touring group organized by Lloyd Carlton. She describes how students traveled to perform in southern Idaho schools, gaining valuable experience and independence, and exposing communities to various dance forms.

lines.line: # Changes in Dance Community Camaraderie

> Barbara reflects on the decline of camaraderie among dance teachers, noting a shift toward competition. She contrasts the collaborative spirit of earlier years with the current environment, expressing nostalgia for the supportive relationships that once characterized the dance community.

metadata.title: Changes in Dance Community Camaraderie
metadata.synopsis: Barbara reflects on the decline of camaraderie among dance teachers, noting a shift toward competition. She contrasts the collaborative spirit of earlier years with the current environment, expressing nostalgia for the supportive relationships that once characterized the dance community.

lines.line: # College Majors and Further Education

> Barbara explains the limitations of college dance programs during her time, her parents' reluctance to let her major in ballet, and her eventual degrees in political science and nonprofit management. She describes the University of Utah's unique program for arts management and its eventual discontinuation.

metadata.title: College Majors and Further Education
metadata.synopsis: Barbara explains the limitations of college dance programs during her time, her parents' reluctance to let her major in ballet, and her eventual degrees in political science and nonprofit management. She describes the University of Utah's unique program for arts management and its eventual discontinuation.

lines.line: # Longevity, Health, and Advice for Dancers

> Barbara shares her advice for maintaining health and longevity as a dancer, emphasizing the importance of continual movement and strengthening exercises. She offers practical tips for keeping feet and ankles strong, especially for students preparing for pointe work.

metadata.title: Longevity, Health, and Advice for Dancers
metadata.synopsis: Barbara shares her advice for maintaining health and longevity as a dancer, emphasizing the importance of continual movement and strengthening exercises. She offers practical tips for keeping feet and ankles strong, especially for students preparing for pointe work.

lines.line: # Boise Music Week History and Impact

> Barbara recounts the origins and evolution of Boise Music Week, describing its growth from outdoor performances to a week-long event with themed nights and free musical productions. She highlights the community's volunteer spirit and the significance of Music Week in Boise's cultural life.

metadata.title: Boise Music Week History and Impact
metadata.synopsis: Barbara recounts the origins and evolution of Boise Music Week, describing its growth from outdoor performances to a week-long event with themed nights and free musical productions. She highlights the community's volunteer spirit and the significance of Music Week in Boise's cultural life.

lines.line: # Idaho Dance Arts Alliance and Summer Workshops

> Barbara discusses the Idaho Dance Arts Alliance, its efforts to unite dance teachers statewide, and the creation of intensive summer workshops for students. She describes the logistics, locations, and collaborative spirit that made these workshops successful and beneficial for Idaho's dance community.

metadata.title: Idaho Dance Arts Alliance and Summer Workshops
metadata.synopsis: Barbara discusses the Idaho Dance Arts Alliance, its efforts to unite dance teachers statewide, and the creation of intensive summer workshops for students. She describes the logistics, locations, and collaborative spirit that made these workshops successful and beneficial for Idaho's dance community.

lines.line: # Suggestions for Further Oral Histories

> Barbara suggests other candidates for oral history interviews, such as Kay Braden, and describes their backgrounds and contributions to dance. She highlights the diversity of experiences among local teachers and the value of capturing their stories for posterity.

metadata.title: Suggestions for Further Oral Histories
metadata.synopsis: Barbara suggests other candidates for oral history interviews, such as Kay Braden, and describes their backgrounds and contributions to dance. She highlights the diversity of experiences among local teachers and the value of capturing their stories for posterity.

lines.line: # Other Notable Dance Figures in Boise

> Barbara and the interviewer discuss other notable dance figures in Boise, including Kay Mac and Helene Petersen, their teaching roles, and their impact on the community. They reflect on the importance of recreational dance programs and the varied paths of local dancers.

metadata.title: Other Notable Dance Figures in Boise
metadata.synopsis: Barbara and the interviewer discuss other notable dance figures in Boise, including Kay Mac and Helene Petersen, their teaching roles, and their impact on the community. They reflect on the importance of recreational dance programs and the varied paths of local dancers.

lines.line: # Tap Dance Experiences and Local Teachers

> Barbara shares her experiences with tap dance, her struggles with the style, and her interactions with local tap teachers. The conversation highlights the interconnectedness of the dance community and the opportunities for adults to continue dancing.

metadata.title: Tap Dance Experiences and Local Teachers
metadata.synopsis: Barbara shares her experiences with tap dance, her struggles with the style, and her interactions with local tap teachers. The conversation highlights the interconnectedness of the dance community and the opportunities for adults to continue dancing.

lines.line: # Encouragement for More Interviews and Final Thoughts

> The interviewer encourages Barbara to conduct further interviews and explains the simplicity of the recording process. They express gratitude for Barbara's contributions and discuss the importance of documenting Boise's rich dance history and talent.

metadata.title: Encouragement for More Interviews and Final Thoughts
metadata.synopsis: The interviewer encourages Barbara to conduct further interviews and explains the simplicity of the recording process. They express gratitude for Barbara's contributions and discuss the importance of documenting Boise's rich dance history and talent.

lines.line: # Closing Remarks

> The interview concludes with mutual appreciation and reflections on the joy of sharing memories. The participants express their enjoyment of the conversation and prepare to end the recording, marking the close of the oral history session.

metadata.title: Closing Remarks
metadata.synopsis: The interview concludes with mutual appreciation and reflections on the joy of sharing memories. The participants express their enjoyment of the conversation and prepare to end the recording, marking the close of the oral history session.

story.title: Call with Barbara Wescott
story.description: The interview with Barbara Westcott, conducted as part of Idaho's America 250 celebration, delves into the rich dance history of Treasure Valley. Barbara discusses her dance journey, beginning with her early ballet training in Boise under Lloyd Carlton, sparked by a performance of Ballet Russe. Despite an injury ending her performance aspirations, she pivoted to teaching, establishing Dance Arts Academy and influencing many dancers, including her daughter Ann Westcott, a professional. The conversation highlights Boise's vibrant dance culture, significant historical contributors, and collaborations like the Boise Dance Collection and Idaho Dance Arts Alliance, showcasing the community's dedication to nurturing dance arts.

participants.name: Barbara Rice Wescott and Aleta Sales Pottenger
